title: Vault Authority Hijack via Reinitialization
summary: Accounts initialized without Anchor's 8-byte discriminator can be reinitialized by anyone who controls the account key. An attacker who previously owned an account (or can create it with zero balance) can call initialize again and reset the vault authority to their key, gaining full control.

rent_exemption.note: Attacker only needs rent-exempt balance if creating new account; for pre-existing accounts this is 0

scenario_a: 0 SOL (account previously existed, lamports returned to attacker on close)
Attacker previously had account, closed it (lamports returned), then reinitializes

scenario_b: Attacker creates account with minimum balance, then reinitializes

complexity.explanation: Requires understanding of Anchor discriminator mechanism and Solana rent system. Mechanical steps are straightforward: call initialize with attacker key as authority.

notes: Requires access to the vault account's keypair (or account that was previously closed)
Common in programs with user-specific PDAs where users can close accounts
Harder to detect - legitimate-looking initialize call resets authority

preconditions: Attacker controls the vault account keypair OR vault account was previously closed and refunded
Vault was initialized without Anchor's #[account] attribute (missing discriminator)
No reinit guard exists (no manual discriminator check)

scenario_a_account_reuse.description: Attacker previously had a vault account, closed it (lamports returned), and now reinitializes it

steps.action: Create vault account with attacker's authority
steps.result: Vault created with attacker authority, attacker knows vault PDA
Lamports returned to attacker, vault account data zeroed, account now empty but address exists
No discriminator check - vault reinitialized with victim's authority

scenario_b_fresh_account.description: Attacker creates a new account at a known address and initializes it

steps.action: Create account at vault PDA address
steps.note: Attacker must know the PDA derivation seeds to generate the address

steps.action: Wait for legitimate users to deposit

mechanics.root_cause: Missing #[account] attribute on VaultState struct means Anchor doesn't write 8-byte discriminator on init or verify it on load
mechanics.safe_code:
#[account]
pub struct VaultState { ... }  // Anchor adds discriminator
mechanics.anchor_behavior: #[account] generates: (1) 8-byte discriminator on init, (2) discriminator verification on load, (3) reinit detection
mechanics.discriminator_mechanism: Anchor uses first 8 bytes of account data as discriminator, computed as BorshSerialize(type_name). When no discriminator exists (all zeros), any data is accepted as valid.

on_chain_signals: initialize called on account that already has data
initialize called with different authority than original
Vault authority change without authorized instruction
off_chain_signals: Unexpected initialize events for existing accounts
Authority changes in program event logs
code_analysis: Verify all account types have #[account] or manual discriminator check
Check for init constraint on Account<'info, T> where T has #[account]

remediation.alternative: Add manual discriminator check before initialization
remediation.defense_in_depth: Combine with authority verification and event emission
remediation.migration_note: Existing accounts need one-time migration to add discriminator

related_attacks.pattern: Reinitialization attacks are common in programs that use init_if_needed without discriminator checks

poc_verification.expected_behavior: Second initialize should FAIL with AlreadyInitialized after fix
poc_verification.test_scenario: Call initialize twice with different authorities - second should fail
